GraphDocument(nodes=[Node(id='demo_project_123', type='Project', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123'}), Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', type='Document', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'source': 'tim_cook_bio.txt', 'title': 'Tim Cook Biography', 'created_at': '2025-11-01 00:00:00Z'}), Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', type='Document', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'source': 'tim_cook_bio.txt', 'title': 'Tim Cook Biography', 'created_at': '2025-11-01 00:00:00Z'}), target=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. properties={'name': 'Timothy Donald Cook', 'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), type='MENTIONS', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': properties={'name': 'Timothy Donald Cook', 'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), type='MENTIONS', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. properties={'name': 'Steve Jobs', 'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), type='MENTIONS', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': properties={'name': 'Timothy Donald Cook', 'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), type='MENTIONS', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': properties={'name': 'Timothy Donald Cook', 'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), type='MENTIONS', Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. properties={'name': 'Timothy Donald Cook', 'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), type='MENTIONS', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': properties={'name': 'Timothy Donald Cook', 'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), type='MENTIONS', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': properties={'name': 'Timothy Donald Cook', 'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), type='MENTIONS', Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': properties={'name': 'Timothy Donald Cook', 'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), type='MENTIONS', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source': 'Apple', 'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), type='MENTIONS', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0'}), Relationship(source=Node(id='055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', type='Chunk', properties={'project_id': 'demo_project_123', 'doc_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7', 'chunk_id': '055341f9811b4b952a4601093ab2a7d7_0', 'text': "\nTimothy Donald Cook (born November 1, 1960) is an American business executive who has been \nthe chief executive officer (CEO) of Apple since 2011. He had previously been the company's chief \noperating officer under its co-founder Steve Jobs. Cook joined Apple in March 1998 as a senior vice \npresident for worldwide operations, and then as vice president for worldwide sales and operations. \nHe was appointed chief executive of Apple on August 24, 2011, after Jobs resigned. \nDuring his tenure as the chief executive of Apple and while serving on its board of directors, \nhe has advocated for the political reform of international and domestic surveillance, \ncybersecurity, national manufacturing, and environmental preservation. Since becoming CEO, \nCook has also replaced Jobs' micromanagement with a more liberal style and implemented a \ncollaborative culture at Apple. Since 2011 when he took over Apple, to 2020, Cook doubled \nthe company's revenue and profit, and the company's market value increased from $348 billion \nto $1.9 trillion. In 2025, Apple was the second largest technology company by revenue, \nwith US$416 billion. Outside of Apple, Cook has sat on the board of directors of Nike since 2005. \nHe also sits on the board of the National Football Foundation and is a trustee of Duke University, \nhis alma mater. Cook engages in philanthropy; in March 2015 he said he planned to donate his fortune \nto charity. In 2014, Cook became the first and only chief executive of a Fortune 500 company \nto publicly come out as gay. In October 2014, the Alabama Academy of Honor inducted Cook, \nwho spoke on the state's record of LGBTQ rights. It is the highest honor Alabama gives \nits citizens. In 2012 and 2021, Cook appeared on the Time 100, Time's annual list of the 100 \nmost influential people in the world. As of December 2025, his net worth is estimated at $2.6 billion, \naccording to Forbes. Early life and education  Cook was born on November 1, 1960, \nin the city of Mobile, Alabama. He was baptized in a Baptist church and grew up in the nearby \ncity Robertsdale. His father, Donald Cook, was a shipyard worker. His mother, Geraldine Cook, \nworked at a pharmacy. Cook graduated salutatorian from the public Robertsdale High School in \nAlabama in 1978. Cook received a Bachelor of Science with a major in industrial engineering \nfrom Auburn University in 1982 and a Master of Business Administration from Duke University in 1988.\n", 'source':
